g.data-narration: Frame buffer is the core of modern display systems, storing pixel data for screen display.
A frame buffer is dedicated memory storing complete image data. Each pixel's color is mapped directly to screen positions.
g.text: A dedicated video memory array that stores pixel values representing
the complete image to be displayed on screen.

g.data-narration: The frame buffer is organized as a 2D array matching screen resolution. Each pixel holds RGB or RGBA color data.
g.text: 24-bit color: 8 bits each for R, G, B | 32-bit: adds 8-bit Alpha channel

g.data-narration: Screen coordinates start at top-left (0,0), with Y increasing downward—different from math Cartesian coordinates.
g.text: Screen coords: Origin (0,0) at TOP-LEFT, X→ right, Y↓ downward
Math coords: Origin at center/bottom, Y↑ upward—DIFFERENT from screen!

g.data-narration: To set a pixel, calculate memory offset: offset = y × width + x. Then multiply by bytes per pixel for actual address.
g.text: offset = y × width + x | For 640×480 buffer, pixel (100,200) → offset = 128,100
Then multiply by bytes-per-pixel for actual memory address in the frame buffer.

g.data-narration: RGB model uses Red, Green, Blue channels (0-255 each). Pure colors: Red=(255,0,0), White=(255,255,255).
g.text: Each pixel has R, G, B values (0-255). 24-bit: 8 bits per channel

g.data-narration: RGB values pack into 32-bit integers: packed = (α<<24)|(R<<16)|(G<<8)|B. This enables efficient memory operations.
DDA (Digital Differential Analyzer) uses incremental calculation to determine pixel positions along a line using floating-point math.
g.text: Digital Differential Analyzer: increments along longer axis
x(k+1) = x(k) + dx/steps, y(k+1) = y(k) + dy/steps
Steps = max(|dx|, |dy|) for the line from (x1,y1) to (x2,y2)

g.data-narration: For line from (2,2) to (10,6), DDA calculates: dx=8, dy=4, steps=8, then increments x by 1, y by 0.5 each step.
Bresenham's algorithm uses only integer arithmetic for efficiency. It calculates a decision parameter to choose optimal pixels.
g.text: Uses ONLY integer arithmetic - FASTER than DDA
Initial p = 2×dy - dx | If p<0: p += 2×dy else: p += 2×dy - 2×dx
Decision parameter determines whether to step Y or stay flat

g.data-narration: For line (2,2) to (10,6): p starts at 0. Trace through: plot each pixel, update p based on sign.
Midpoint circle algorithm exploits 8-way symmetry: draw one octant and reflect to get the rest—8x faster!
g.text: Uses 8-way symmetry: Draw ONE octant, mirror to get 7 more!
p = x² + y² - r² | Decision at midpoint determines next pixel

g.data-narration: Scanline fill processes polygons row-by-row, finding edge intersections and filling horizontal spans between them.
g.text: Scanline fill: Process each row, find where scanline intersects polygon edges
Fill horizontal spans between intersection pairs. Use edge tables for optimization.
Flood fill: Start from seed point, fill outward until hitting boundary color

g.data-narration: Anti-aliasing reduces jagged edges by blending pixel intensities based on distance from ideal primitive edges.
g.text: Aliasing: Jagged stair-step edges | Anti-aliasing: Smooth edges via intensity modulation
Supersampling: Render at higher res, downsample | Wu's algorithm: Calculate intensity based on distance
Alpha channel stores transparency for proper compositing and blending

g.data-narration: Remember: screen coords have Y downward, offset = y×W+x, Bresenham uses integer math, circle uses 8-way symmetry.
g.text: • Screen coords: Y increases DOWNWARD from top-left origin
• Memory offset: offset = y × width + x (multiply by bytes-per-pixel)
• Bresenham: Integer-only, faster than DDA | Circle: 8-way symmetry
